questions.question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun. It is also the smallest planet in our solar system.
questions.funFact: Mercury has no atmosphere, so it has no weather!

questions.question: What is the name of the star closest to Earth?
questions.explanation: The Sun is our closest star. It gives us light and warmth every day.
questions.funFact: The Sun is so big that about 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it!

questions.question: How many planets are there in our solar system?
questions.explanation: There are 8 planets in our solar system. Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
questions.funFact: You can remember the planets in order with: My Very Excited Mother Just Served Us Nachos!

questions.question: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
questions.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet because its surface is covered with reddish dust and rocks.
questions.funFact: Mars has the tallest volcano in the solar system called Olympus Mons!

questions.question: What shape is the Earth?
questions.explanation: Earth is roughly a sphere, slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator.
questions.funFact: From space, Earth looks blue because of all the water on its surface!

questions.question: What does the Moon orbit around?
questions.explanation: The Moon orbits around the Earth. It takes about 27 days to complete one orbit.
questions.funFact: The Moon is the only place beyond Earth where humans have walked!

questions.question: Which planet has big beautiful rings around it?
questions.explanation: Saturn is famous for its bright and beautiful rings made of ice and rock.
questions.funFact: Saturn is so light that if you could find a bathtub big enough, it would float in water!

questions.question: What do we call a person who travels to space?
questions.explanation: A person who travels to space is called an astronaut. In India, they are also called vyomanauts.
questions.funFact: Astronauts grow about 2 inches taller in space because gravity does not squeeze their spine!

questions.question: Which is the biggest planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system. It is so big that all other planets could fit inside it.
questions.funFact: Jupiter has a giant storm called the Great Red Spot that has been raging for hundreds of years!

questions.question: What colour does the Sun appear to us during the day?
questions.explanation: The Sun appears white-yellow to us during the day. It is actually white but our atmosphere makes it look slightly yellow.
questions.funFact: Never look directly at the Sun because its light is so strong it can hurt your eyes!

questions.question: Which planet do we live on?
questions.explanation: We live on planet Earth. It is the third planet from the Sun.
questions.funFact: Earth is the only planet in our solar system that has liquid water on its surface!

questions.question: What lights up the Moon at night?
options: The Moon makes its own light
The Sun's light reflects off it
questions.explanation: The Moon does not make its own light. It shines because sunlight bounces off its surface.
questions.funFact: The Moon has dark spots called 'seas' but they have no water at all!

questions.question: What do we call the group of stars that form a pattern in the sky?
questions.explanation: A constellation is a group of stars that form a recognisable pattern in the sky, like a connect-the-dots picture.
questions.funFact: There are 88 official constellations in the sky, and people have been naming them for thousands of years!

questions.question: Which planet is the hottest in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Venus is the hottest planet even though Mercury is closer to the Sun. Venus has thick clouds that trap heat.
questions.funFact: Venus is so hot that it could melt lead!

questions.question: What is a shooting star actually?
options: A piece of rock burning in the atmosphere
questions.explanation: A shooting star is actually a small piece of space rock that burns up as it enters Earth's atmosphere.
questions.funFact: Millions of shooting stars happen every day, but most are too small to see!

questions.question: Does the Sun move around the Earth or does the Earth move around the Sun?
options: The Sun moves around the Earth
The Earth moves around the Sun
They move around each other equally
questions.explanation: The Earth orbits around the Sun. It takes one full year for Earth to complete one orbit.
questions.funFact: The Earth travels around the Sun at about 107,000 kilometres per hour!

questions.question: What causes day and night on Earth?
questions.explanation: Day and night happen because the Earth rotates on its axis. The side facing the Sun has daytime while the other side has nighttime.
questions.funFact: One full spin of the Earth takes about 24 hours, which is why we have 24 hours in a day!

questions.question: What is the name of our galaxy?
questions.explanation: Our galaxy is called the Milky Way. It contains billions of stars including our Sun.
questions.funFact: The Milky Way got its name because it looks like a milky band of light across the night sky!

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Pluto is no longer classified as a planet. In 2006, scientists reclassified it as a dwarf planet.
questions.funFact: Pluto is smaller than Earth's Moon!

questions.question: What do astronauts wear when they go outside a spacecraft?
questions.explanation: Astronauts wear spacesuits to protect them in space. Spacesuits provide air, keep the right temperature, and protect from harmful radiation.
questions.funFact: A spacesuit costs about 12 million US dollars to make!

questions.question: What is the name of India's space agency?
questions.explanation: ISRO stands for Indian Space Research Organisation. It was founded in 1969 and is headquartered in Bengaluru.
questions.funFact: ISRO launched 104 satellites in a single mission in 2017, setting a world record at that time!

questions.question: What was the name of India's Mars mission?
questions.explanation: Mangalyaan, also called the Mars Orbiter Mission, was India's first mission to Mars launched in 2013. India became the first country to reach Mars orbit on its first attempt.
questions.funFact: Mangalyaan cost less than the budget of many Hollywood movies!

questions.question: What did the Chandrayaan-3 mission achieve in 2023?
options: Landed near the Moon's south pole
questions.explanation: Chandrayaan-3 successfully soft-landed near the Moon's south pole in August 2023. India became the first country to land near the lunar south pole.
questions.funFact: The Chandrayaan-3 rover is named Pragyan, which means 'wisdom' in Sanskrit!

questions.question: What are asteroids mostly found between in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Most asteroids are found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter. This region contains millions of rocky objects.
questions.funFact: The largest asteroid, Ceres, is so big it is also classified as a dwarf planet!

questions.question: What is the International Space Station (ISS)?
options: A laboratory orbiting Earth where astronauts live
A rocket launchpad in the ocean
questions.explanation: The ISS is a large spacecraft that orbits Earth. Astronauts live and work there conducting scientific experiments.
questions.funFact: The ISS orbits Earth about 16 times every single day, which means astronauts see 16 sunrises and sunsets daily!

questions.question: Who was the first Indian to travel to space?
questions.explanation: Rakesh Sharma became the first Indian citizen to travel to space in 1984 aboard the Soviet Soyuz T-11 mission.
questions.funFact: When asked how India looked from space, Rakesh Sharma famously replied, 'Saare Jahan Se Achha!'

questions.question: What is a comet's tail made of?
options: Gas and dust released by the comet
questions.explanation: A comet's tail is made of gas and dust that stream off as it gets closer to the Sun and heats up.
questions.funFact: A comet's tail always points away from the Sun, no matter which direction the comet is moving!

questions.question: Which planet rotates on its side, almost like a rolling ball?
questions.explanation: Uranus is tilted so far on its axis that it appears to roll around the Sun on its side. Its tilt is about 98 degrees.
questions.funFact: Because of its extreme tilt, some parts of Uranus get 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of darkness!

questions.question: What is a satellite?
options: An object that orbits a planet or star
questions.explanation: A satellite is any object that orbits around a larger body. The Moon is a natural satellite of Earth, and we also have thousands of human-made satellites.
questions.funFact: There are over 7,000 active satellites orbiting Earth right now!

questions.question: How long does it take sunlight to reach Earth?
questions.explanation: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth, travelling at 300,000 kilometres per second.
questions.funFact: This means when you look at the Sun, you are actually seeing it as it was 8 minutes ago!

questions.question: What is the name of the first human to walk on the Moon?
questions.explanation: Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.
questions.funFact: Neil Armstrong's footprints are still on the Moon because there is no wind or rain to wash them away!

questions.question: Which planet has the most moons in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Saturn has the most known moons with over 140 discovered so far, surpassing Jupiter's count.
questions.funFact: Saturn's moon Titan is bigger than the planet Mercury and has its own thick atmosphere!

questions.question: What type of galaxy is the Milky Way?
questions.explanation: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy. It has a central bar-shaped structure with spiral arms extending outward.
questions.funFact: It would take about 100,000 years travelling at the speed of light to cross the Milky Way from one side to the other!

questions.question: What does ISRO's Aditya-L1 mission study?
questions.explanation: Aditya-L1 is India's first space mission dedicated to studying the Sun. It was launched in September 2023.
questions.funFact: Aditya means 'Sun' in Sanskrit, and L1 refers to the Lagrange point 1, a special spot in space between Earth and the Sun!

questions.question: What makes up the rings of Saturn?
questions.explanation: Saturn's rings are made up of billions of pieces of ice and rock, ranging in size from tiny grains to house-sized chunks.
questions.funFact: Even though Saturn's rings stretch out very wide, they are incredibly thin, sometimes only about 10 metres thick!

questions.question: Why do astronauts float inside the International Space Station?
options: There is no gravity in space
They are in free fall around Earth
questions.explanation: Astronauts float because the ISS and everyone inside it are in a constant state of free fall around Earth. This creates the feeling of weightlessness.
questions.funFact: Gravity on the ISS is actually about 90% as strong as on Earth's surface, but you do not feel it because you are falling!

questions.question: What is the first human-made object to leave our solar system?
questions.explanation: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, became the first human-made object to enter interstellar space in 2012.
questions.funFact: Voyager 1 carries a Golden Record with sounds and images from Earth, just in case aliens find it!

questions.question: Which planet spins the fastest in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Jupiter rotates the fastest of all the planets. One day on Jupiter is only about 10 hours long.
questions.funFact: Jupiter spins so fast that it bulges out at its equator, making it look slightly squashed!

questions.question: What is a light year?
options: The time it takes light to travel in one year
The distance light travels in one year
A year on a planet near a star
questions.explanation: A light year is the distance that light travels in one year, which is about 9.46 trillion kilometres. It is a unit of distance, not time.
questions.funFact: The nearest star to our Sun, Proxima Centauri, is about 4.24 light years away!

questions.question: What happens at the centre of a black hole?
options: A point of infinite density called a singularity
questions.explanation: At the centre of a black hole is a singularity, a point where matter is thought to be infinitely dense and the laws of physics as we know them break down.
questions.funFact: The supermassive black hole at the centre of our Milky Way galaxy is called Sagittarius A* and has a mass of about 4 million Suns!

questions.question: What is the event horizon of a black hole?
options: The centre of the black hole
The boundary beyond which nothing can escape
The ring of light around it
The area where stars are born
questions.explanation: The event horizon is the boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing, not even light, can escape its gravitational pull.
questions.funFact: If you could watch someone falling into a black hole, they would appear to slow down and freeze at the event horizon due to time dilation!

questions.question: What force keeps planets in orbit around the Sun?
questions.explanation: Gravity is the force that keeps planets in orbit around the Sun. The Sun's massive gravity pulls on all objects in the solar system.
questions.funFact: Gravity travels at the speed of light, so if the Sun suddenly disappeared, Earth would continue orbiting for about 8 minutes before flying off in a straight line!

questions.question: What is a neutron star?
options: A star made entirely of neutrons left after a supernova
A star that produces no light
A star orbiting a black hole
questions.explanation: A neutron star is the collapsed core of a massive star that has exploded as a supernova. It is incredibly dense and composed mostly of neutrons.
questions.funFact: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 6 billion tonnes on Earth!

questions.question: What is redshift in astronomy?
options: When stars turn red before dying
The stretching of light from objects moving away from us
When planets appear red through telescopes
questions.explanation: Redshift occurs when light from an object moving away from us gets stretched to longer, redder wavelengths. It is key evidence that the universe is expanding.
questions.funFact: Edwin Hubble used redshift to discover that galaxies are moving away from us, which led to the Big Bang theory!

questions.question: What is the Kuiper Belt?
options: A ring of asteroids between Mars and Jupiter
A region of icy bodies beyond Neptune's orbit
A band of satellites around Earth
A dust cloud near the Sun
questions.explanation: The Kuiper Belt is a vast region of icy bodies and dwarf planets that extends beyond the orbit of Neptune. Pluto is one of its most famous members.
questions.funFact: The Kuiper Belt is estimated to contain hundreds of thousands of icy objects larger than 100 kilometres across!

questions.question: What is the cosmic microwave background radiation?
options: The afterglow of the Big Bang
Radiation from nuclear reactors in space
questions.explanation: The cosmic microwave background radiation is the faint glow of light left over from the early universe, about 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
questions.funFact: About 1% of the static you see on an old untuned television is actually caused by cosmic microwave background radiation!

questions.question: What is the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram used for?
options: Classifying stars by luminosity and temperature
questions.explanation: The Hertzsprung-Russell diagram plots stars according to their luminosity and surface temperature. It helps astronomers understand stellar evolution.
questions.funFact: Most stars, including our Sun, fall on a band called the Main Sequence on the HR diagram, where they spend most of their lives!

questions.question: What is dark matter?
options: Matter that is very dark in colour
Invisible matter that exerts gravitational effects
questions.explanation: Dark matter is a mysterious form of matter that does not emit or absorb light but exerts gravitational effects on visible matter. It makes up about 27% of the universe.
questions.funFact: Scientists estimate that dark matter and dark energy together make up about 95% of the universe, and we still do not fully understand either of them!

questions.question: What is a supernova?
options: The explosive death of a massive star
questions.explanation: A supernova is the powerful explosion that occurs when a massive star reaches the end of its life. It can briefly outshine an entire galaxy.
questions.funFact: The heavy elements in your body, like iron in your blood and calcium in your bones, were all created inside exploding stars billions of years ago!

questions.question: What is the observable universe's estimated age?
questions.explanation: The universe is estimated to be about 13.8 billion years old, based on observations of the cosmic microwave background radiation and the expansion rate of the universe.
questions.funFact: The observable universe is about 93 billion light years across, which is much larger than 13.8 billion light years because space itself has been expanding!

questions.question: Why can we not hear any sounds in space?
options: There is no air for sound to travel through
Our ears do not work in space
questions.explanation: Sound needs air or some material to travel through. Space is a vacuum with almost no air, so sound waves cannot travel there.
questions.funFact: Even if a huge star exploded right next to you in space, you would not hear a single thing!

questions.question: What is the shape of the Moon when it looks like a big bright circle?
questions.explanation: When the Moon looks like a complete bright circle in the sky, it is called a Full Moon. This happens when the Sun fully lights up the side of the Moon facing Earth.
questions.funFact: A Full Moon happens about once every 29 days, and some months have two Full Moons. The second one is called a Blue Moon!

questions.question: What do rockets need to escape Earth and fly into space?
options: Very powerful engines that push them up really fast
questions.explanation: Rockets use powerful engines that burn fuel to create a huge push called thrust. This thrust must be strong enough to overcome Earth's gravity.
questions.funFact: A rocket must travel faster than 40,000 kilometres per hour to escape Earth's gravity!

questions.question: Which planet is closest in size to Earth and sometimes called Earth's twin?
questions.explanation: Venus is almost the same size as Earth, which is why it is sometimes called Earth's twin. But it is very different because it is extremely hot and has thick toxic clouds.
questions.funFact: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus! It takes Venus longer to spin once than to orbit the Sun.

questions.question: What do stars look like when you see them twinkling at night?
questions.explanation: Stars look like tiny twinkling points of light because they are extremely far away. They are actually giant balls of hot gas, but the great distance makes them appear very small.
questions.funFact: Stars do not actually twinkle! They appear to twinkle because Earth's atmosphere bends their light as it passes through.

questions.question: What is the Moon covered with?
questions.explanation: The Moon's surface is covered with grey dust and has many craters made by space rocks crashing into it over billions of years.
questions.funFact: The footprints left by astronauts on the Moon will stay there for millions of years because there is no wind or rain to wipe them away!

questions.question: What makes the Sun so bright and hot?
options: It is on fire like a campfire
It is a giant light bulb
It makes energy by squeezing tiny atoms together
questions.explanation: The Sun produces energy through a process where tiny atoms of hydrogen are squeezed together to form helium. This releases a huge amount of light and heat.
questions.funFact: The temperature at the centre of the Sun is about 15 million degrees Celsius! That is much hotter than any fire on Earth.

questions.question: Which is the smallest planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the smallest planet in our solar system. It is only a little bigger than Earth's Moon.
questions.funFact: Mercury has no moons at all! It is one of only two planets in our solar system without any moons.

questions.question: What is the Great Red Spot on Jupiter?
options: A giant storm that has lasted hundreds of years
questions.explanation: The Great Red Spot is a massive storm on Jupiter that has been raging for at least 350 years. It is so large that Earth could fit inside it.
questions.funFact: Winds inside the Great Red Spot can reach speeds of over 640 kilometres per hour, which is faster than any tornado on Earth!

questions.question: What is the difference between an asteroid and a comet?
options: Asteroids are rocky while comets are made of ice and dust
Comets are found closer to the Sun
There is no difference between them
questions.explanation: Asteroids are mainly made of rock and metal, while comets are made of ice, dust, and frozen gases. When comets get close to the Sun, the ice heats up and creates a glowing tail.
questions.funFact: Some comets take thousands of years to complete a single orbit around the Sun!

questions.question: What was the name of NASA's space telescope launched in 2021 to replace the Hubble?
questions.explanation: The James Webb Space Telescope was launched in December 2021. It is the most powerful space telescope ever built and can look deeper into space than Hubble.
questions.funFact: The James Webb Space Telescope has a gold-coated mirror that is 6.5 metres wide, and it orbits the Sun about 1.5 million kilometres from Earth!

questions.question: What is ISRO's Gaganyaan mission designed to do?
questions.explanation: Gaganyaan is India's first crewed spaceflight programme. It aims to send Indian astronauts, called vyomanauts, into orbit around Earth.
questions.funFact: Gaganyaan means 'Sky Vehicle' in Sanskrit! India would become only the fourth country to independently send humans into space.

questions.question: Which planet has the longest day in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Venus has the longest day of any planet. It takes about 243 Earth days for Venus to complete one full rotation on its axis.
questions.funFact: Venus also rotates in the opposite direction to most other planets, so if you could stand on Venus, the Sun would rise in the west and set in the east!

questions.question: What are the two tiny moons of Mars called?
questions.explanation: Mars has two small moons called Phobos and Deimos. They are much smaller than our Moon and are thought to be captured asteroids.
questions.funFact: Phobos is slowly getting closer to Mars and scientists think it will either crash into Mars or break apart into a ring in about 50 million years!

questions.question: What is the name of the force that pushes a rocket upward?
questions.explanation: Thrust is the force created by a rocket's engines that pushes it upward. The engines burn fuel and push hot gas downward, which in turn pushes the rocket in the opposite direction.
questions.funFact: The Saturn V rocket, which carried astronauts to the Moon, produced 34.5 million newtons of thrust at launch, more than 30 times the force of a jumbo jet!

questions.question: What is a space probe?
options: An unmanned spacecraft sent to explore space
questions.explanation: A space probe is a robotic spacecraft that is sent to explore planets, moons, and other objects in space without any crew on board. It sends data back to scientists on Earth.
questions.funFact: NASA's New Horizons probe travelled for over 9 years and 5 billion kilometres to reach Pluto and send back the first close-up photos!

questions.question: What is the life cycle of a star like our Sun?
options: Nebula, main sequence star, red giant, white dwarf
Main sequence star, supernova, neutron star
Nebula, blue giant, supernova, black hole
questions.explanation: A Sun-like star is born in a nebula, spends most of its life as a main sequence star, then swells into a red giant, and finally sheds its outer layers to become a white dwarf.
questions.funFact: Our Sun is currently about halfway through its main sequence life and has about 5 billion more years before it becomes a red giant!

questions.question: What is an exoplanet?
options: A planet that has been destroyed
A planet that orbits a star outside our solar system
A dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt
A planet made entirely of gas
questions.explanation: An exoplanet is a planet that orbits a star other than our Sun. Thousands of exoplanets have been discovered, and some are in the habitable zone where liquid water could exist.
questions.funFact: The first exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star was discovered in 1995, and since then over 5,000 exoplanets have been confirmed!

questions.question: According to Einstein's theory of special relativity, what happens to time for an object moving close to the speed of light?
options: Time slows down relative to a stationary observer
questions.explanation: According to special relativity, time passes more slowly for objects moving at speeds close to the speed of light compared to a stationary observer. This is called time dilation.
questions.funFact: GPS satellites have to account for time dilation! Their clocks tick slightly faster than clocks on Earth because of both their speed and weaker gravity.

questions.question: What is the Hubble Space Telescope primarily used for?
options: Observing distant galaxies, nebulae, and stars in visible and ultraviolet light
Searching for radio signals from aliens
questions.explanation: The Hubble Space Telescope orbits Earth and captures incredibly detailed images of distant galaxies, nebulae, and stars. Being above the atmosphere gives it much clearer views than ground telescopes.
questions.funFact: Hubble has made over 1.5 million observations since its launch in 1990 and has helped scientists publish over 19,000 research papers!

questions.question: What is dark energy?
options: A mysterious force causing the expansion of the universe to accelerate
questions.explanation: Dark energy is a mysterious force that is causing the expansion of the universe to speed up rather than slow down. It makes up about 68% of the total energy content of the universe.
questions.funFact: Dark energy was discovered in 1998 when scientists noticed that distant supernovae were dimmer than expected, meaning the universe's expansion is accelerating!

questions.question: What is the Chandrasekhar limit?
options: The maximum distance a telescope can see
The maximum mass a white dwarf star can have before it collapses
The edge of the observable universe
The closest a spacecraft can get to the Sun
questions.explanation: The Chandrasekhar limit is about 1.4 times the mass of our Sun. If a white dwarf exceeds this mass, it can no longer support itself and may explode as a Type Ia supernova.
questions.funFact: This limit is named after Indian-American astrophysicist Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar, who calculated it when he was just 19 years old!

questions.question: What is a pulsar?
options: A planet that pulses with light
A rapidly spinning neutron star that emits beams of radiation
questions.explanation: A pulsar is a rapidly rotating neutron star that emits beams of electromagnetic radiation from its magnetic poles. As it spins, the beams sweep across space like a lighthouse.
questions.funFact: Some pulsars spin hundreds of times per second! The fastest known pulsar rotates 716 times per second.

questions.question: What is gravitational lensing?
options: A lens used in space telescopes
The bending of light around a massive object due to gravity
A technique for focusing laser beams in space
The reflection of light off planetary rings
questions.explanation: Gravitational lensing occurs when the gravity of a massive object like a galaxy cluster bends the path of light from a more distant object behind it, acting like a magnifying glass.
questions.funFact: Einstein predicted gravitational lensing in 1936, and astronomers have used it to discover some of the most distant galaxies ever observed!

questions.question: What is the habitable zone around a star?
options: The area where astronauts can survive without spacesuits
The range of orbits where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface
The region where asteroids cannot enter
The zone closest to the star where it is warmest
questions.explanation: The habitable zone, also called the Goldilocks zone, is the region around a star where conditions are just right for liquid water to exist on a planet's surface, which is considered essential for life.
questions.funFact: Earth sits comfortably in our Sun's habitable zone, but if it were just 5% closer or farther, conditions for life might be very different!
